- Location: back (less critical than face/hands/feet/genitalia); (2) **Burn Center Referral Criteria (ABA)** — refer to burn center if: - Partial-thickness > 10% TBSA - Full-thickness any size (especially face, hands, feet, perineum, joints) - Chemical, electrical burns - Inhalation injury - Special needs (preexisting medical conditions, traumatic injury) - Pediatric/elderly - Inadequate social/psychological support - Indications: outpatient mgmt may be appropriate for small partial-thickness, but deep + isolation = consider admission; (3) **Outpatient management** for small partial-thickness burns: - Wound care: cleanse with mild soap + water, gentle debridement of loose skin, topical agents (silver sulfadiazine — controversial in modern; alternative silver-based dressings, bacitracin, mupirocin for small areas); - Dressings: non-adherent, change daily or per dressing type; - Pain control: oral analgesic, scheduled - Tetanus prophylaxis update - Follow-up clinic 48-72h then weekly; (4) **Surgical management** for full-thickness / deep partial: - Early excision + grafting (5-7 days) — improves outcome vs delayed; - Split-thickness skin graft from donor site (thigh) - Alternative: dermal regenerative templates (Integra) for full-thickness without immediate autograft availability; (5) **Hospitalization indicated** for: large/deep burns, poor social support (inability to do wound care, transportation), special locations, comorbidities, inhalation, infection signs, severe pain not controlled, age extremes; (6) **Nutritional + rehabilitation** — for larger / deep burns; (7) **Long-term**: scarring (hypertrophic, keloid), contracture (physical therapy, splinting), pruritus, psychological (PTSD), pigmentation; (8) **Burn types**: thermal (most common), scald, contact (this case), chemical, electrical, radiation, friction; (9) **Special considerations**: - Heating pad burns: often deep due to prolonged contact + low temp + lack of pain feedback (especially in DM neuropathy, alcoholic, drug overdose, falling asleep) - Suspect deeper than appears; (10) **Abuse / neglect** consideration if circumstances suspicious — pediatric, elderly, dependent adult — investigation + reporting

explanation: **Burn management** — outpatient vs hospitalization. **Burn Center Referral Criteria** (American Burn Association — ABA): 1. Partial-thickness burns > 10% TBSA (any age) 2. Burns involving face, hands, feet, genitalia, perineum, major joints 3. Full-thickness burns any size in any age 4. Electrical burns including lightning 5. Chemical burns 6. Inhalation injury 7. Burn patients with preexisting medical disorders complicating mgmt 8. Burn + concomitant trauma 9. Burn injuries in children + hospitals without qualified personnel/equipment 10. Burns requiring special social/psychological/long-term rehabilitative support **Outpatient burn care criteria**: - Small (< 10% TBSA) partial-thickness in adults - No special area involvement - Patient/caregiver capable of wound care - Adequate access to follow-up - No significant comorbidities **Outpatient wound care**: 1. Cleansing: mild soap + water, irrigation 2. Debridement: gentle, loose dead skin 3. Topical: - Silver sulfadiazine (SSD) — traditional; concerns about pseudo-eschar, delayed healing; rarely used in modern practice - Silver-based dressings (Aquacel Ag, Mepilex Ag) — less frequent changes - Bacitracin / mupirocin — small areas - Hydrogels, hydrocolloid — superficial 4. Dressings: non-adherent, foam, antimicrobial 5. Pain control 6. Tetanus prophylaxis 7. Follow-up 48-72h, then weekly **Surgical management for deep/full-thickness**: - **Early excision + grafting** (5-7 days) — improves outcomes; reduces hospital stay, complications, scarring - **Split-thickness skin graft** — donor: thigh, back; allows healing both sites - **Dermal regenerative templates** (Integra, AlloDerm) — full-thickness without donor; second-stage thin skin graft - **Cultured epithelial autograft (CEA)** — extensive burns; lab-grown - **Allograft (cadaveric)** — temporary coverage extensive burns - **Pediatric**: similar principles; pay attention to growth + scar contracture **Heating pad / contact burns** — special considerations: - Prolonged low-grade heat → deep tissue damage despite small surface area - Often patient unaware (sleeping, drugs, neuropathy, intoxication) - Higher rate of full-thickness than expected - Common in DM with peripheral neuropathy → counsel against use - Abuse consideration in vulnerable populations.
reference_source: ABA Burn Center Referral Criteria; ISBI Practice Guidelines for Burn Care 2016; Strock LL et al. J Burn Care Res

scenario:
Basic science: surgical pathology + tumor markers

คำถาม: tumor markers in clinical practice

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Tumor markers — uses + limitations**: (1) **Roles**: - Diagnosis support (rarely sufficient alone) - Staging adjunct - Prognosis - Treatment monitoring - Surveillance for recurrence; (2) **Limitations**: most have low sensitivity (miss disease) + specificity (false positives from non-malignant conditions); not screening tools (except in selected — PSA controversially in selected men); (3) **Common surgical tumor markers**: a. **CEA (Carcinoembryonic Antigen)**: - Colorectal — most useful application; baseline + post-resection + surveillance (rising = recurrence) - Also elevated in: smoking, IBD, hepatitis, other GI cancers, breast, lung b. **CA 19-9**: - Pancreatic cancer — most useful; surveillance + response - Also: cholangiocarcinoma, gallbladder, gastric, colorectal; - Lewis-negative blood group (5-10%) → false-negative c. **CA 125**: - Ovarian — surveillance + response - Also: endometriosis, pregnancy, menstruation, peritonitis, ascites, pleural effusion, other GI/lung cancers d. **CA 15-3, CA 27.29**: - Breast cancer — metastatic monitoring (not for primary screening) e. **AFP (Alpha-fetoprotein)**: - HCC, non-seminoma germ cell tumor - Hepatocellular Ca: surveillance in cirrhosis + diagnosis support (LI-RADS) - Testicular GCT: elevation = NSGCT (even if pure seminoma histology) f. **β-hCG**: - Trophoblastic disease (gestational, choriocarcinoma) - Germ cell tumor (some) - Pregnancy normal g. **PSA (Prostate-Specific Antigen)**: - Prostate Ca screening (controversial — overdiagnosis), monitoring - Elevated also: BPH, prostatitis, instrumentation, ejaculation h. **Calcitonin**: - Medullary thyroid Ca — secretes; baseline + surveillance - Elevated in: C-cell hyperplasia, MEN2 family screening, ESRD i. **Chromogranin A**: - Neuroendocrine tumors — pheochromocytoma, carcinoid, gastrinoma, neuroendocrine pancreatic - Also elevated: PPI use, ESRD, severe HF, atrophic gastritis j. **Thyroglobulin (Tg)**: - Differentiated thyroid Ca (PTC, FTC) — post-thyroidectomy surveillance marker; high anti-Tg Ab may interfere k. **LDH**: - Germ cell tumors, lymphoma — prognostic + response l. **5-HIAA**: - Carcinoid (urine 24h) m. **Catecholamines + metanephrines**: - Pheochromocytoma — plasma free metanephrine most sensitive (~ 99%); n. **Inhibin B, AMH**: - Ovarian sex cord-stromal tumors o. **HE4 + CA 125 + ROMA**: - Ovarian Ca risk algorithm; (4) **Liquid biopsy / circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA)** — emerging: - Treatment response monitoring - Resistance mutations detection (e.g., EGFR T790M in NSCLC) - Surveillance for MRD (minimal residual disease) - Stage I/II colorectal — MRD detection + adjuvant decision; (5) **Interpretation principles**: - Use trends, not single value - Combine with imaging + clinical context - Disease-specific cutoffs + validation - Don't overinterpret minor fluctuations - Confounders: liver disease, smoking, inflammation, ESRD, gender, age

explanation: **Tumor markers** — biological substances elevated in malignancy; useful for diagnosis support, staging, prognosis, treatment monitoring, surveillance. Almost never diagnostic alone — combined with imaging + biopsy + clinical context. **Major tumor markers** with primary surgical use: 1. **CEA** — colorectal (surveillance), also breast, lung, GI; baseline + post-op + q3-6 mo × 5 yr 2. **CA 19-9** — pancreatic, cholangio, gastric; Lewis-negative blood group (5-10%) → no CA 19-9 production 3. **CA 125** — ovarian; many false positives (endometriosis, menstruation, pregnancy, peritoneal inflammation) 4. **CA 15-3 / CA 27.29** — breast metastatic monitoring 5. **AFP** — HCC + non-seminoma GCT 6. **β-hCG** — trophoblastic + GCT 7. **PSA** — prostate 8. **Calcitonin** — medullary thyroid Ca 9. **Thyroglobulin** — differentiated thyroid Ca post-thyroidectomy surveillance 10. **Chromogranin A** — neuroendocrine 11. **LDH** — GCT, lymphoma 12. **5-HIAA** (urine) — carcinoid 13. **Metanephrines** — pheochromocytoma **Interpretation principles**: - Trend over time more valuable than single value - Disease-specific cutoffs + validation - Half-life informs interpretation (AFP 5-7d, hCG 24-36h) - Confounders: smoking elevates CEA, liver disease elevates many, ESRD chromogranin, gender + age **Liquid biopsy (ctDNA, CTCs)** — emerging: - Tumor genotyping non-invasively - Detection of resistance mutations (e.g., EGFR T790M, HER2 amplification) - MRD (minimal residual disease) detection post-resection — adjuvant therapy decision (e.g., DYNAMIC trial colorectal) - Surveillance — earlier recurrence detection - Limitations: shedding variable, sensitivity, cost **Caveats**: tumor markers should NOT be used for general population screening (except selected — PSA controversial in some; AFP + US for HCC in cirrhotic); false positives + overdiagnosis common in low-prevalence settings.
reference_source: NCCN Tumor Marker Guidelines; ASCO Tumor Marker Guidelines; Locker GY et al. JCO

scenario:
Basic science: anesthesia + airway management

คำถาม: difficult airway management

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Difficult airway management — ASA Algorithm**: (1) **Pre-op assessment** — predict difficult airway: - **LEMON criteria**: Look (anatomy, beard, dentition, scars), Evaluate 3-3-2 (3 fingers mouth, 3 fingers chin-hyoid, 2 fingers hyoid-thyroid), Mallampati (I-IV — class III-IV difficult), Obstruction (mass, infection, edema, FB), Neck mobility - Mallampati: I (full uvula visible), II (partial), III (only soft palate), IV (only hard palate) - Thyromental distance: < 6 cm = difficult - Sternomental: < 12.5 cm difficult - History of difficult intubation - Prior surgery, RT, anatomy abnormalities - OSA, obesity (BMI > 30), pregnancy, beard; (2) **ASA Difficult Airway Algorithm**: - Awake intubation if predicted difficult (preferred — patient breathing) - Asleep approach if airway unlikely difficult; (3) **Awake fiberoptic intubation (AFOI)** — gold standard for predicted difficult: - Topical anesthesia (lidocaine 4% — atomized, gargle, transtracheal, glossopharyngeal block) - Antisialagogue (glycopyrrolate) - Minimal sedation (dexmedetomidine ideal — preserves respiration; remifentanil; midazolam moderate) - Flexible bronchoscope intubation - Awake provides safety net; (4) **Direct laryngoscopy (DL)** — traditional; Macintosh or Miller blade; Cormack-Lehane view I-IV; difficult if III-IV; (5) **Video laryngoscopy (VL)** — increasingly first-line: - GlideScope, McGRATH, C-MAC - Better view especially anterior larynx - May not improve intubation success if technique not adapted - First-line in pediatric some centers; (6) **Supraglottic airway (LMA)** — rescue + primary in selected: - LMA Classic, LMA ProSeal, LMA Supreme, i-gel, LMA Fastrach (intubating LMA) - Bridge to intubation, surgical airway, emergency oxygenation; (7) **Combitube / King LT** — emergency airway; pre-hospital + difficult airway rescue; (8) **Surgical airway** — failed intubation + cannot ventilate: - **Cricothyroidotomy** — fastest; through cricothyroid membrane; needle (transtracheal) or open surgical - **Tracheostomy** — definitive but slower; emergent if cricothyroidotomy not feasible (children, anatomy distortion); (9) **'Cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate (CICO)'** scenario: - Surgical airway is mandated - Train and equipment readily available; (10) **Special situations**: - Anaphylaxis with angioedema - Burn / inhalation injury - Trauma C-spine immobilized - Pediatric (different anatomy — larger tongue, more anterior larynx, large occiput) - Pregnant (Mallampati increases, edema, aspiration risk) - Morbidly obese; (11) **Post-intubation**: confirm with capnography (end-tidal CO2 — gold standard), auscultation, CXR

explanation: **Difficult Airway Management** — critical clinical skill spanning anesthesia + emergency + critical care + trauma surgery. **Pre-op assessment**: 1. **History**: prior difficult intubation, surgery, radiation, OSA, snoring, ankylosing spondylitis, RA, obesity, pregnancy 2. **Examination**: - Mallampati class (I-IV) - Thyromental distance (≥ 6 cm) - Sternomental distance - Mouth opening (≥ 3 cm) - Neck mobility - Mandibular protrusion - Dentition, beard, anatomy 3. **LEMON mnemonic**: Look, Evaluate 3-3-2, Mallampati, Obstruction, Neck **ASA Difficult Airway Algorithm (2022 update)**: - Predicted difficult → awake intubation considered first - Anticipated difficult → optimize positioning, equipment, expertise, plan B/C/D - Unanticipated difficult → ramp-up tools + call for help **Awake fiberoptic intubation (AFOI)**: gold standard for predicted difficult; topical local anesthesia + minimal sedation + flexible bronchoscope. **Video laryngoscopy (VL)**: increasingly first-line; better view for anterior larynx; AIRWAYS-2 trial showed mixed results but generally favorable. **Supraglottic airway (LMA)**: rescue + bridge; not definitive in vomiting/regurgitation; primary in selected (short procedures, ambulatory). **Bougie + stylet** — adjuncts. **Surgical airway** — failed intubation + cannot ventilate (CICO scenario): - **Cricothyroidotomy** — preferred emergency: needle (transtracheal jet ventilation — temporary 30 min); surgical (open) — definitive - **Tracheostomy** — definitive; slower; difficult in emergency; pediatric — high tracheal can be safer **Pediatric airway differences**: large tongue, more anterior + cephalad larynx (C3-C4 vs C5-C6 adult), funnel-shaped airway (narrow at cricoid until age 8), large occiput (positioning), softer cartilages. **Trauma**: C-spine precautions (collar, in-line stabilization), bag-mask + jaw thrust without head tilt, video laryngoscopy advantageous. **Confirmation post-intubation**: end-tidal CO2 (capnography) — gold standard; auscultation; chest rise; CXR (depth + position). **Crew Resource Management**: closed-loop communication, role assignment, calling for help early, debriefing.
reference_source: ASA Difficult Airway Management Guidelines 2022; DAS UK Guidelines; Apfelbaum JL et al. Anesthesiology

scenario:
Basic science: fluid + electrolyte management

คำถาม: postoperative IV fluid + electrolyte management

choices.label: A
choices.text: Liberal IV fluid for all postop

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Postoperative fluid management — goal-directed restrictive trend**: (1) **Fluid types**: - **Crystalloid**: balanced solutions (Lactated Ringer's, Plasma-Lyte) preferred over 0.9% NSS for resuscitation (avoid hyperchloremic acidosis); 5% dextrose for hypoglycemia adjunct; - **Colloid**: albumin (5% for resuscitation in selected — septic shock per Surviving Sepsis 2021); HES (hydroxyethyl starch) no longer recommended (renal injury + mortality); gelatins; - **Blood products** for specific indications; (2) **Goal-directed fluid therapy (GDT)**: - Avoid both over-resuscitation (third spacing, edema, ileus, anastomotic, ARDS) AND under-resuscitation (organ hypoperfusion); - Dynamic measures of fluid responsiveness: passive leg raise (PLR), pulse pressure variation (PPV), stroke volume variation (SVV), POCUS; - CVP no longer recommended as fluid responsiveness predictor; - Targets: MAP > 65, lactate clearance, UO 0.5 mL/kg/hr (caution in over-resuscitating just to UO); (3) **ERAS principles** for fluid: - Pre-op: no prolonged NPO (clear fluid up to 2h), carb loading 2h pre-op (improves insulin sensitivity); - Intra-op: balanced solutions, restrictive but adequate; avoid 'over-the-top' fluid; - Post-op: oral intake as soon as tolerated; minimize maintenance IV; goal euvolemia; (4) **RELIEF trial (Myles NEJM 2018)** — restrictive (low) vs liberal (higher) fluid in major abdominal surgery: restrictive associated with more AKI; modern recommendation: moderate (not extreme either way) goal-directed; (5) **Electrolyte management postop**: - **Sodium**: monitor; both hyponatremia (overhydration with hypotonic) + hypernatremia possible - **Potassium**: monitor; K losses from NG, diarrhea, diuretic, alkalosis; replete carefully (max 10 mEq/h peripheral, 20 mEq/h central with monitoring) - **Magnesium**: often low postop; replete (Mg essential for K + Ca regulation, neuromuscular, cardiac) - **Calcium**: hypocalcemia post-thyroid/parathyroid surgery; transfusion (citrate); replete - **Phosphate**: refeeding syndrome, depletion; (6) **Glycemic control**: target 140-180 (avoid both extreme hyper + hypoglycemia); insulin sliding scale + correction; long-acting basal + bolus for outpatient regimens; (7) **Acid-base disorders postop**: - Metabolic acidosis: lactate (perfusion), hyperchloremia (NSS over-use), ketone (NPO, DKA) - Metabolic alkalosis: NG suction, vomiting, diuretic - Respiratory: hypoventilation (opioid, pain, sedation), hyperventilation (anxiety, pain, sepsis); (8) **Special situations**: - Cardiac surgery — pulmonary edema risk - Major liver resection — coagulopathy, hypoalbumin, ascites - Bariatric — dumping, dehydration - Sepsis — initial resuscitation 30 mL/kg crystalloid then goal-directed; (9) **Monitoring**: weight daily (most accurate volume change), I/O, electrolytes daily or more, hemodynamics, end-organ perfusion (UO, lactate, mental status)

explanation: **Perioperative fluid + electrolyte management** — fundamental + has evolved significantly. Major shift from 'liberal' to 'restrictive moderate' (goal-directed). **Crystalloid selection**: - **Balanced (LR, Plasma-Lyte)** preferred over **0.9% NaCl** (normal saline) for resuscitation - NS large volumes → hyperchloremic acidosis, AKI risk (SMART, SALT-ED trials) - LR contains some K (4 mEq/L) — caution in hyperkalemia; safe in most - D5W for hypoglycemia/hypernatremia adjunct **Colloid use**: - **Albumin** — selected (e.g., large volume paracentesis, hepatorenal syndrome, septic shock per Surviving Sepsis; no general benefit over crystalloid per SAFE trial) - **HES (hydroxyethyl starch)** — NOT recommended (CHEST, 6S trials — increased renal injury + mortality) - **Gelatins** — limited use, expensive, no superior outcomes **Goal-directed therapy**: avoid both extremes; dynamic measures preferred over static (CVP, urine output alone). **ERAS fluid principles**: - No prolonged NPO (clear fluid up to 2h) - Carb loading 2h pre-op - Moderate intraoperative (balanced solutions) - Early oral intake postop - Minimize maintenance IV when oral OK **Restrictive vs liberal** — RELIEF trial (Myles NEJM 2018): restrictive fluid in major abdominal surgery associated with more AKI vs slightly liberal; modern consensus: moderate goal-directed (not extreme either way). **Electrolyte considerations postop**: - Na: monitor; hyponatremia from hypotonic fluid + SIADH common postop; hypernatremia from dehydration - K: NG losses, diuretic; replete carefully - Mg: low common; impacts K + Ca + cardiac; replete IV - Ca: post-thyroid/parathyroid; transfusion (citrate) - PO4: refeeding syndrome **Refeeding syndrome**: severely malnourished resuming feeds → PO4 + Mg + K drop into cells; cardiac + neuromuscular dysfunction; thiamine deficiency manifests. Prevent: gradual feed advancement + thiamine pre-feed + electrolyte monitoring + replacement. **Acid-base**: assess Anion Gap; common postop disorders: metabolic acidosis (lactic, hyperchloremic), metabolic alkalosis (NG, vomiting), respiratory acidosis (hypoventilation). **Glycemic**: target 140-180; hyperglycemia + hypoglycemia both bad; use insulin sliding scale + correction. **Special**: cardiac surgery (pulmonary edema risk; restrictive); liver resection (coagulopathy, ascites, hypoalbumin); bariatric (dumping, dehydration risk).
reference_source: ERAS Society Fluid Therapy; Myles PS et al. NEJM 2018 (RELIEF); Semler MW et al. NEJM 2018 (SMART); Surviving Sepsis 2021

scenario:
Basic science: cardiac arrest + cardiopulmonary resuscitation in surgical patient

คำถาม: post-cardiac surgery cardiac arrest — special protocol

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Cardiac Arrest after Cardiac Surgery (CALS) Protocol — modified ACLS**: (1) **Critical differences from standard ACLS**: - Recent thoracotomy + sternotomy + intracardiac/great vessel anatomy - Resternotomy with internal cardiac massage if external CPR fails - Specific arrest etiologies — VF, tamponade, bleeding, ischemia, hypovolemia, pacemaker; (2) **Immediate response within 5 minutes** (modified ACLS): - Confirm arrest - Call for help + arrest team + perfusionist (CPB on standby) - Begin chest compressions (caution with sternotomy site / wires) - Defibrillate VF/VT (3 shocks before drug if witnessed in monitored ICU) - Pacing if asystole / bradycardia + pacemaker available (post-op patients often have epicardial wires); (3) **Specific etiologic management**: - **Tamponade** — open chest within 5 min if PEA / ineffective CPR (life-saving) - **Hemorrhage** — open chest, identify + control source, transfuse - **Tension pneumothorax** — decompress - **Hypovolemia** — fluid + blood - **Hyperkalemia** — Ca + insulin + glucose - **MI / graft thrombosis** — emergent intervention; (4) **Resternotomy**: - Indications: VF/VT unresponsive 3 shocks, PEA without reversible cause, asystole, suspected tamponade - Within 5 min of arrest to maximize survival - Internal cardiac massage 100/min, defibrillation internal, identify cause - All ICUs caring for post-cardiac surgery patients should have resternotomy capability; (5) **Drugs in CALS**: - Avoid epinephrine bolus initially (can cause severe HTN + intracardiac complications) - Use vasopressin / norepi infusion - Amiodarone, lidocaine for VF/VT; (6) **Extracorporeal life support (ECMO / ECLS)**: - VA-ECMO bridge for refractory - Cannulation femoral + peripheral or central - Improved outcomes with rapid deployment; (7) **Outcomes** with CALS protocol — survival higher (50%+) than standard ACLS in cardiac surgery patients due to addressing surgical etiology + resternotomy; (8) **Training + drills** — STS CALS course + simulation; ICU + OR teams should be familiar; (9) **Documentation + debrief**: M&M, learnings, prevention; family communication

explanation: **Cardiac Arrest after Cardiac Surgery (CALS)** — distinct protocol per Society of Thoracic Surgeons (STS) due to unique pathophysiology + therapeutic opportunities. **Why different from standard ACLS**: - Specific etiologies (tamponade 35%, hemorrhage 25%, VF/VT 25%, MI/graft, tension pneumothorax, hyperkalemia, pacemaker failure) - Recent thoracotomy + sternotomy enables internal cardiac massage + visualization - Standard chest compressions may injure surgical site - Epicardial pacing wires often available - Goal: identify + treat specific cause within 5 min to optimize outcome **Key steps (STS CALS Protocol)**: 1. Within 1 min: confirm arrest, call for help (arrest team + perfusion) 2. VF/VT (witnessed monitored ICU): 3 shocks immediately, then chest compressions 3. Asystole / severe bradycardia: pace with epicardial wires if available 4. PEA: think of reversible causes (tamponade, tension pneumothorax, hypovolemia, hyperkalemia) 5. **Resternotomy within 5 min** if: - VF/VT after 3 shocks - PEA without reversible cause identified - Persistent arrest > 5 min - Suspected tamponade or active bleeding 6. Internal cardiac massage during open exploration 7. Identify + treat cause: control bleeding, evacuate tamponade, repair injury, ECMO if cardiogenic shock **Drug differences**: - Avoid bolus epinephrine (severe HTN, intracardiac complications) - Use vasopressin or norepi infusion - Amiodarone for VF/VT - Calcium for hyperkalemia / Ca channel blocker toxicity **ECMO / ECLS**: bridge to recovery / transplant; rapid deployment improves survival in refractory. **Outcomes**: CALS protocol implementation improves survival from cardiac arrest after cardiac surgery to ~ 50%+ (vs 17% standard ACLS). **Training**: STS publishes CALS course + simulation curriculum; ICU + OR teams should be familiar + drilled.
reference_source: STS Cardiac Surgical Resuscitation (CALS) Protocol; Dunning J et al. Eur J Cardiothorac Surg; Society of Thoracic Surgeons

scenario:
ระบบ EMS / quality / business of medicine: medical malpractice + surgical complications

คำถาม: how to handle adverse outcome + communication

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Adverse event management + communication — open disclosure + patient safety**: (1) **Immediate response**: - Address ongoing medical care first (treat patient, stabilize, escalate) - Document accurately + factually; (2) **Open disclosure / 'Sorry Works'**: - Communicate transparently with patient + family - Express empathy + concern (apology for distress experienced, not necessarily admission of fault) - Explain what happened in understandable language - Discuss what investigation will be undertaken - Discuss plan for ongoing care + how to prevent recurrence - Avoid speculation + blame; - Evidence: open disclosure REDUCES litigation + improves trust (Michigan Open Disclosure, COPIC, Kaiser studies); (3) **Apology laws / 'I'm sorry' laws** — many jurisdictions (~ 39 US states, multiple international) protect expressions of empathy from being used as admission of liability; check local; (4) **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** — systematic event analysis: - Multidisciplinary team - Timeline reconstruction - Identify contributing factors (people, process, equipment, environment, communication) - 'Just culture' — system vs individual - Develop action plan - Implement + monitor; (5) **M&M Conferences** — surgical tradition: - Case-based learning - Educational + safety improvement - Confidential (peer review protection) + non-punitive in best practice; (6) **Quality improvement processes**: - Failure Mode + Effects Analysis (FMEA) — proactive risk assessment - PDSA cycles (Plan-Do-Study-Act) - Lean / Six Sigma methodology; (7) **Documentation principles**: - Contemporaneous + factual - Avoid speculation, blame, emotion - Late entries marked + dated - Original records preserved; (8) **Legal considerations** when adverse event: - Notify risk management / hospital administration - Consult legal counsel - Maintain communication with patient (open disclosure within legal framework) - Quality of care matters AND communication matters — both reduce litigation - 'Disclose + offer' programs (e.g., University of Michigan) — proactive disclosure + apology + compensation — reduces overall claims + costs; (9) **Patient safety culture**: - Just culture - Blameless reporting (incident reports, near-miss reporting) - Leadership commitment - Continuous learning; (10) **Personal impact**: - Second victim phenomenon — physician/team also affected by adverse outcome - Peer support, mental health support, debriefing - Burnout prevention

explanation: **Adverse event management + open disclosure** — central to modern patient safety + culture. **Open disclosure** (Australia, UK, US trends — Joint Commission, AHA, ACOG advocate): 1. **Acknowledge** the event happened 2. **Empathize** + offer sincere apology for impact 3. **Explain** in understandable terms what happened (factually, without speculation) 4. **Discuss** investigation + steps to prevent recurrence 5. **Continue** care + communication 6. **Compensation** if appropriate **Evidence**: open disclosure programs (Michigan, COPIC, Kaiser, VA): - Decreased litigation + costs - Improved patient satisfaction - Improved staff morale - Improved safety culture **Apology laws**: ~ 39 US states + many international jurisdictions protect expressions of empathy/sympathy from being used as admission of liability. Allows physicians to apologize without legal jeopardy. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** principles: - System focus, not individual blame - Identify contributing factors (5 Whys, Ishikawa fishbone) - Generate corrective actions - Implement + monitor - 'Just culture' — distinguish system error vs individual reckless behavior vs honest mistake **'Just culture'** (Marx, Reason): - Three behaviors: human error (console), at-risk (coach), reckless (sanction) - Encourages reporting + learning - Removes fear of blame for honest mistake **Failure Mode + Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — proactive: anticipate failure modes before they occur; mitigate risk. **PDSA cycle** (Plan-Do-Study-Act) — quality improvement framework. **M&M Conference** — surgical learning tradition; case-based; peer review protection; educational not punitive in optimal practice. **Documentation** in adverse events: factual + contemporaneous + non-judgmental; avoid speculation, opinion, blame; legal protection by clear documentation. **Second victim phenomenon** — clinician/team distress after adverse event affecting patient: physical, psychological, professional impact; peer support, debriefing, mental health resources critical; programs like RISE (Resilience in Stressful Events) at Johns Hopkins. **Disclose + Offer Programs** (University of Michigan, others) — proactive disclosure of error + apology + compensation offer — reduces overall claims, costs, and patient harm.
reference_source: Joint Commission Sentinel Event Reporting; AHRQ Open Disclosure; ACOG Committee Opinion; Boothman RC et al. (Michigan Open Disclosure)

scenario:
ระบบ EMS / professionalism: ethics + informed consent

คำถาม: surgical informed consent + complex situations

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Informed consent — ethical + legal cornerstone**: (1) **Elements of informed consent**: - **Disclosure**: nature of procedure, indication, risks (material risks), benefits, alternatives (including no treatment), prognosis - **Comprehension**: patient understands (assess with teach-back) - **Voluntariness**: not coerced - **Competence/Capacity**: decision-making capacity present - **Authorization**: explicit consent; (2) **Standards for disclosure** (vary by jurisdiction): - **Professional / reasonable physician**: what a reasonable physician would disclose (older standard) - **Reasonable patient (material risk)**: what a reasonable patient would want to know — modern preferred standard - **Subjective patient**: what this specific patient would want; (3) **Capacity assessment** — 4 components (MacArthur): - Understand information - Appreciate situation + consequences - Reason about options - Communicate choice; (4) **Capacity vs competence**: - Capacity is clinical judgment, decision-specific, fluctuates - Competence is legal determination - Both required for valid consent; (5) **Special situations**: - **Emergency**: implied consent for life-saving treatment when capacity absent + no surrogate available - **Surrogate decision-maker**: legal hierarchy (spouse, adult child, parent, sibling per state law); follow advance directive / patient's prior expressed wishes (substituted judgment); best interests if no expressed wishes - **Pediatric**: parent / legal guardian; assent of child as developmentally appropriate; exceptions: emancipated minor, mature minor doctrine, specific medical issues (STI, contraception, mental health, substance use depending on jurisdiction) - **Pregnancy**: maternal autonomy paramount; competing fetal interests legally + ethically complex - **Cognitive impairment**: assess capacity decision-by-decision; involve family + surrogate; advance directives - **Jehovah's Witness + blood refusal**: respect adult capacitated patient; pediatric — court intervention may be needed (best interest of child); document + plan accordingly; (6) **Documentation**: signed consent form + procedure note documenting discussion; teach-back; questions answered; (7) **Refusal of treatment**: equally valid right; document discussion + understanding; offer second opinion + reconsider; counsel of consequences without coercion; (8) **Withdrawal / withholding life-sustaining treatment**: same ethical principle as not initiating; supported by ethics committees, palliative care, legal frameworks; symptom management during withdrawal (opioid + benzodiazepine for dyspnea/anxiety); not euthanasia; (9) **Cultural + religious considerations**: explore + respect; interpreter services; family-centered decision-making in some cultures; (10) **Ethics consultation** for complex/disputed: hospital ethics committee resource; (11) **Conflicts of interest**: disclose; research consent additional layer; IRB oversight

explanation: **Informed Consent** — ethical + legal foundation of surgical practice. Required for all surgical procedures (with limited exceptions like emergent life-saving in unconscious patient). **Four elements**: 1. **Disclosure** — adequate information 2. **Comprehension** — patient understands 3. **Voluntariness** — free of coercion 4. **Authorization** — explicit consent **Disclosure standards** (jurisdiction-dependent): - **Professional / reasonable physician**: traditional — what reasonable physician would disclose - **Material risk / reasonable patient** (Canterbury v. Spence 1972, US): modern preferred — what reasonable patient would want to know; includes common risks + serious uncommon risks - **Subjective patient**: rare — what this particular patient would want **Capacity** — clinical determination, decision-specific, fluctuating: - Understand information - Appreciate situation + consequences - Reason about options - Communicate choice MacArthur Competence Assessment Tool — standardized. **Competence** — legal determination (court). **Special situations**: 1. **Emergency** — implied consent for life-saving in incapacitated patient without available surrogate; treatment to extent reasonable + necessary 2. **Surrogate decision-making** — hierarchy by state law: typically spouse, adult child, parent, sibling, other relative, close friend; follow advance directive if exists; use substituted judgment (what patient would want); best interests if no expressed wishes 3. **Pediatric** — parental consent + age-appropriate assent; emancipated minor, mature minor doctrine, specific conditions (reproductive, mental health, substance use) — varies by state 4. **Cognitive impairment / dementia** — capacity decision-by-decision; advance directives; surrogate 5. **Blood refusal (Jehovah's Witness)** — adult capacitated patient's right; pediatric — best interest of child may override (court intervention if needed) 6. **Pregnancy** — maternal autonomy paramount; some legal cases test 7. **Substance use, intoxication** — capacity assessment carefully 8. **Mental illness** — does not equate to incapacity; assess specifically **Refusal of treatment** — same right as consent; document + counsel; not coercion. **Withdrawing / withholding life-sustaining treatment** — ethically same as not initiating; well-established legal + ethical framework; symptom management. **Cultural + religious sensitivity** — interpreter, family involvement, cultural humility. **Ethics consultation** — for complex / disputed cases. **Conflicts of interest** disclosure — relevant to consent (research, financial relationships). **Research consent** — IRB + additional protections (HIPAA, informed consent specific to research).
reference_source: AMA Code of Medical Ethics; ACS Statement on Principles; Canterbury v. Spence 1972; MacArthur Competence Assessment

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 25 ปี Type 1 DM uncontrolled HbA1c 11.4 + multiple admit DKA + history of acute MI 1 year ago + ESRD on dialysis 2 years

Referral for kidney + pancreas transplant evaluation

คำถาม: pre-transplant workup + integrative considerations

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Simultaneous Pancreas-Kidney (SPK) transplant evaluation — integrative approach**: (1) **Candidacy assessment for SPK**: - Type 1 DM with ESRD - Brittle DM with poor quality of life (hypoglycemia unawareness, severe complications) - No major medical/surgical contraindications - Adequate functional status to undergo surgery + immunosuppression - Psychological + social support; (2) **Pre-transplant evaluation comprehensive**: - **Cardiac**: stress test (often pharmacologic — adenosine, dobutamine); coronary angiogram given prior MI (recurrent ischemia risk); echo (LV function, valves); 24h Holter if arrhythmia history - **Pulmonary**: PFT; CXR - **Infectious**: HIV, HBV, HCV, CMV, EBV, VZV, TB (IGRA/skin test), syphilis, strongyloides (depending on region); vaccination updates - **Cancer screening**: age-appropriate (colonoscopy, mammogram, Pap, PSA), thorough skin exam - **Endocrine**: thyroid, parathyroid (HPT common in ESRD) - **GI**: hepatitis serology + LFT; H. pylori; - **Vascular**: peripheral arterial — both for transplant access + future considerations - **Dental**: clearance, source control - **Psychiatric / psychosocial**: depression, anxiety, substance use, adherence assessment, support system - **Nutrition**: optimize - **Smoking cessation absolute**; (3) **Listing for transplant** — UNOS / OPTN matching system; - HLA typing, panel reactive antibodies (PRA), crossmatch - Allocation algorithm by region; - Wait list management; (4) **Surgical considerations**: - Pancreas transplant + kidney transplant — combined operation - Pancreas: portal vs systemic venous drainage; bladder vs enteric exocrine drainage - Kidney: heterotopic in iliac fossa - Iliac vessel access + arterial reconstruction; (5) **Immunosuppression** post-transplant: - Induction: anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG) or basiliximab - Maintenance: tacrolimus + mycophenolate + steroid (taper) - Adjustments per complications - Lifelong; (6) **Outcomes**: - SPK > Pancreas alone in long-term graft survival - 5-year patient survival 90%+, pancreas graft 70-80%, kidney 80-85% - Quality of life: insulin-free, improved DM complications progression - Hypoglycemia unawareness resolved typically; (7) **Post-transplant management**: - Immunosuppression monitoring (drug levels) - Rejection surveillance (creatinine, amylase, glucose) - Infection prophylaxis (PJP — TMP-SMX; CMV — valganciclovir; fungal — fluconazole) - Cancer surveillance (increased risk) - Cardiovascular risk management - Lifestyle - Adherence support; (8) **Family + support involvement** essential; (9) **Long-term complications**: chronic rejection, infections, malignancy (especially skin, PTLD), recurrence of underlying disease (rare in T1DM)

explanation: **Simultaneous Pancreas-Kidney (SPK) Transplantation** — option for selected T1DM with ESRD: free from dialysis + insulin; improved QOL + survival vs dialysis alone or kidney alone for many. **Indications**: 1. T1DM with ESRD (creatinine clearance < 20-30, on dialysis or pre-emptive) 2. Brittle T1DM with hypoglycemia unawareness or severe complications without ESRD (pancreas alone — PA — option, but lower graft survival) **Patient selection considerations**: - Cardiac risk significant in DM patients — comprehensive evaluation - Vascular disease assessment - Psychosocial readiness + adherence - Avoid in active malignancy, active infection, severe non-correctable comorbidity, substance use **Three main pancreas transplant types**: 1. **SPK** (Simultaneous Pancreas-Kidney) — best graft survival of pancreas options 2. **PAK** (Pancreas After Kidney) — if pancreas needs after prior kidney transplant 3. **PA / PTA** (Pancreas Transplant Alone) — for hypoglycemia unawareness without ESRD; lower graft survival **Surgical technique variations**: - **Exocrine drainage**: enteric (most common modern — to Roux limb of jejunum) vs bladder (older — easier monitoring of urinary amylase but UTI + dehydration + acidosis problems) - **Endocrine drainage**: portal (more physiologic) vs systemic (iliac vein — more common) - Concurrent kidney transplant to ipsilateral or contralateral iliac fossa **Immunosuppression** typical: - Induction: ATG (anti-thymocyte globulin) or basiliximab (anti-IL-2R) - Maintenance: tacrolimus + mycophenolate + steroid (often tapered to low-dose or stopped) **Outcomes (USA, UNOS data)**: - 5-yr patient survival ~ 90%+ - 5-yr pancreas graft survival ~ 70-80% - 5-yr kidney graft survival ~ 80-85% - Insulin-free for vast majority while graft functions - Improved diabetic complication progression (nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy) - Resolved hypoglycemia unawareness **Post-transplant management**: - Immunosuppression monitoring + adjustment - Rejection surveillance: creatinine, glucose, amylase (pancreas) - Infection prophylaxis: PJP, CMV, fungal - Vaccine schedule update - Cancer surveillance (skin especially, PTLD) - Cardiovascular risk - Adherence support **Islet cell transplantation** — alternative to whole-organ pancreas; minimally invasive (percutaneous portal vein infusion); good for selected (hypoglycemia unawareness without ESRD); insulin independence rates improving but lower than whole-organ; multiple donors usually needed (CIT protocol). **Newer**: islet auto-transplantation post-pancreatectomy (chronic pancreatitis); stem cell-derived islet therapy (Vertex VX-880 + others in trial).
reference_source: OPTN/UNOS Policies; Gruessner AC + Sutherland DE Pancreas Transplant Outcomes; AST/ASTS Guidelines

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 40 ปี post-living-donor liver transplant 5 ปี for HCC + HBV cirrhosis — มา outpatient clinic for routine surveillance + planning for second pregnancy

Lab: stable graft function, HBV DNA undetectable on antiviral, immunosuppression on low-dose tacrolimus monotherapy

คำถาม: integrative survivorship + reproductive planning

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Post-liver transplant survivorship + planning pregnancy — integrative care**: (1) **Multidisciplinary clinic**: transplant hepatology, transplant surgery, MFM (maternal-fetal medicine), obstetrics, pharmacy, social work, mental health; (2) **Pre-conception counseling**: - Timing: ≥ 1-2 years post-transplant with stable graft function + immunosuppression - Stable lab parameters: normal LFT, stable creatinine, no rejection in 6-12 months - Medication review + adjustment (see below) - Disease control (HBV undetectable, HCC surveillance) - Maternal health optimization (BMI, BP, glycemic, vaccines) - Genetic counseling if disease has heritable component; (3) **Immunosuppression in pregnancy**: - **Tacrolimus** — safe in pregnancy; clearance increases — may need dose increase (monitor levels) - **Cyclosporine** — safe alternative; similar - **Azathioprine** — safe in pregnancy + breastfeeding (despite black box warning, evidence supports safety for fetus) - **Mycophenolate (MMF, MPA)** — TERATOGENIC — fetal loss + congenital anomalies (cleft, ear, cardiac) — STOP 6 weeks before conception + use alternative (azathioprine) - **mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus)** — limited data; preferable to avoid; switch if possible - **Steroids** — low-dose acceptable - **Belatacept** — limited data - **Antivirals (entecavir, tenofovir for HBV)** — tenofovir preferred in pregnancy (better safety data); continue; (4) **HBV-specific considerations**: - Continue antiviral suppression - Tenofovir preferred (TDF or TAF) over entecavir in pregnancy - HBV transmission to neonate: HBIG + HBV vaccine at birth + appropriate maternal viral suppression - Monitor maternal viral load + LFT - HCC surveillance continues; (5) **HCC surveillance**: - 5-yr post-transplant: AFP + imaging (US/CT/MRI) q6-12 mo standard for transplanted HCC - Lifelong surveillance; (6) **Pregnancy management** (high-risk): - Increased risks: HT, GDM, preeclampsia, preterm birth, SGA, miscarriage, abruption, infection - Multidisciplinary high-risk OB - Increased fetal surveillance + maternal monitoring - Vaccinations updated (acellular pertussis Tdap, influenza inactivated) - Avoid live vaccines (yellow fever, MMR, varicella); (7) **Delivery planning**: - Vaginal delivery preferred when feasible - Cesarean for obstetric indications - Anti-rejection medication continued throughout - Stress-dose steroids may be needed - Antibiotic prophylaxis if cesarean (standard); (8) **Postpartum**: - Continue immunosuppression - Lactation: most immunosuppressants compatible (tacrolimus, cyclosporine, azathioprine, prednisone); mycophenolate AVOID - Postpartum surveillance for rejection (immune changes post-delivery) - HCC surveillance resumes per schedule; (9) **Family planning + contraception**: - During fertile years + spacing pregnancies - Reliable methods; IUD (copper preferred over hormonal — controversial efficacy in immunosuppressed; reasonable); barrier; permanent if family complete; (10) **Long-term**: continue lifelong post-transplant care; family + caregiver support; psychological wellbeing

explanation: **Pregnancy after liver transplant** — increasingly common as transplant outcomes improve. **Pre-conception planning**: - ≥ 1-2 years post-transplant - Stable graft function (LFT, Cr, immunosuppression levels) - No rejection within 6-12 months - Disease-specific control (HBV, HCV, autoimmune all in remission) - HCC: appropriate surveillance + acceptable disease-free interval (lifelong surveillance) **Immunosuppression in pregnancy** — major modifications: 1. **Tacrolimus**: CONTINUE; pregnancy-safe; clearance ↑ → may need dose increase (level monitoring) 2. **Cyclosporine**: CONTINUE; alternative to tacrolimus; safe 3. **Azathioprine**: CONTINUE — despite black box warning, evidence supports fetal safety 4. **Prednisone / methylprednisolone**: low-dose CONTINUE; safe 5. **Mycophenolate (MMF, MPA)**: STOP — TERATOGENIC (cleft palate, microtia, congenital heart, fetal loss); switch to azathioprine ≥ 6 weeks pre-conception 6. **mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus)**: limited data; switch off if possible 7. **Belatacept**: limited data **Specific disease considerations**: - **HBV**: continue antiviral suppression; **tenofovir TDF/TAF preferred** in pregnancy (entecavir limited data); newborn HBIG + HBV vaccine; maternal viral load monitoring - **HCV**: cured most patients with DAAs; if active — defer pregnancy until treated - **Autoimmune (PBC, PSC, AIH)**: continue azathioprine; monitor flares - **NASH/MASH**: weight management - **HCC**: continued surveillance regardless of pregnancy **Maternal complications increased**: HT, preeclampsia, GDM, infection, anemia, preterm birth, bleeding (cesarean), graft dysfunction risk. **Fetal complications increased**: prematurity, IUGR/SGA, lower birth weight, miscarriage (some studies); congenital anomalies usually not increased (with appropriate medication management). **Surveillance during pregnancy**: more frequent OB visits, fetal monitoring (growth, biophysical), maternal labs (LFT, Cr, drug levels, glucose, viral load). **Delivery**: vaginal preferred when feasible; cesarean for obstetric indications; routine perioperative antibiotic; anesthesia consult. **Lactation compatibility**: tacrolimus, cyclosporine, azathioprine, prednisone, hydroxychloroquine all generally compatible (modify per Lactmed); mycophenolate AVOID. **Contraception** during transplant + between pregnancies: reliable + safe — IUD (copper or hormonal), barrier, hormonal (caution with drug interactions), permanent if family complete.
reference_source: AST Consensus Conference Pregnancy + Transplantation; AASLD Pregnancy + Liver Disease; Coscia LA et al. Clin Transplant

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 50 ปี post-bariatric sleeve gastrectomy 5 ปีก่อน — มาด้วยอาการ chronic GERD severe + Barrett's segment 2 cm + esophagitis grade C; PPI optimal failed; weight regain 15 kg from nadir

คำถาม: complex management

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Post-sleeve gastrectomy GERD complications + weight regain — multidisciplinary**: (1) **Workup**: - EGD (already done) — confirm Barrett's, biopsy, exclude dysplasia, anatomic assessment (hiatal hernia, sleeve anatomy) - High-resolution manometry — esophageal motility - 24h pH study off PPI — quantify reflux burden - Barium swallow — anatomy of sleeve + reflux dynamics - Possible gastric emptying study; (2) **GERD post-sleeve mechanism**: - Removal of fundus + LES support + His angle disruption - High intragastric pressure (narrow tube) - Hiatal hernia (often pre-existing) - Stenosis at incisura, twisting of sleeve - Decreased gastric compliance; (3) **Medical optimization**: - High-dose PPI (consider esomeprazole 40 mg BID) - H2 blocker bedtime adjunct - Alginate (Gaviscon Advance) post-meal + bedtime - Lifestyle: weight loss if indicated, head of bed elevation, no late meals, avoid trigger foods - Sucralfate for esophagitis; (4) **Barrett's surveillance + management**: - EGD with biopsies (Seattle protocol) q3-5 yr no dysplasia, more frequent if dysplasia - Endoscopic ablation (RFA) for dysplasia/intramucosal carcinoma - PPI lifelong; (5) **Revision bariatric surgery options for severe GERD**: - **Conversion to Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB)** — gold standard for sleeve failure + GERD: eliminates reflux + addresses weight regain + better metabolic outcomes; technically challenging revision - **Magnetic sphincter augmentation (LINX device)** — for selected without hiatal hernia or severe esophagitis; can be done post-sleeve - **Hiatal hernia repair + sleeve adjustment** — if HH primary issue - **Fundoplication impossible** post-sleeve (no fundus); (6) **Weight regain workup** — common after bariatric: - Behavioral (eating habits, portion, snacking, beverages) - Hormonal adaptation (ghrelin recovers, GLP-1 may decrease) - Anatomic (sleeve dilation, gastrogastric fistula) - Psychological (food addiction, binge eating, depression) - Medical (medication weight gain — antidepressants, antipsychotics); (7) **Weight regain management**: - Nutrition + behavioral counseling - Pharmacotherapy: GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide, tirzepatide) — increasingly used post-bariatric; counsel on possible reduced response - Revision surgery if anatomic issue + severe regain - Endoscopic options (re-sleeve, plication) — selected; (8) **Multidisciplinary**: bariatric surgery, GI, nutrition, psychology, primary care; long-term integrated follow-up; (9) **Patient counseling**: realistic expectations, lifelong management

explanation: **Sleeve gastrectomy + GERD** — major issue. Sleeve gastrectomy has GERD as major long-term complication (10-30% develop new or worsened GERD; even higher with longer follow-up). Some develop Barrett's esophagus (~ 3-15% at 5+ years per cohorts), potentially with dysplasia / adenocarcinoma. **Pre-op evaluation should screen for GERD severity** — patients with severe pre-op GERD may not be ideal sleeve candidates; some advocate RYGB primary for these patients. **Mechanisms of post-sleeve GERD**: 1. Anatomic — disruption of His angle, removal of fundus, reduced LES support 2. Physiologic — high intragastric pressure (narrow tube), reduced compliance 3. Hiatal hernia (pre-existing or new) 4. Sleeve anatomy issues — stenosis at incisura, twist, dilated proximal pouch 5. Delayed gastric emptying **Management ladder**: 1. **Medical optimization**: high-dose PPI (potentially BID), H2 blocker adjunct, alginate, lifestyle, weight management 2. **EGD with Barrett's surveillance**: Seattle protocol biopsies; treat dysplasia (RFA/EMR) 3. **Workup**: manometry, pH study, barium swallow, gastric emptying 4. **Surgical revision** for refractory: - **Conversion to RYGB** — most effective for GERD + addresses weight regain; complex revision but acceptable outcomes - **LINX (magnetic sphincter augmentation)** — selected without severe esophagitis or large hiatal hernia; preserves anatomy - **Hiatal hernia repair** if primary anatomic issue 5. **Fundoplication NOT possible** post-sleeve (no fundus available) **Weight regain post-bariatric** — common (20-40% at 10 yr): - Behavioral + dietary - Hormonal adaptation - Anatomic (sleeve dilation, gastrogastric fistula in RYGB) - Psychological - Medication weight gain Management: behavioral counseling, nutrition support, GLP-1 + dual agonists (semaglutide 2.4 mg, tirzepatide 15 mg increasingly used post-bariatric with good response in many), revision surgery for anatomic issue + severe regain, endoscopic options (re-sleeve, plication). **GLP-1 + GIP/GLP-1 dual agonists post-bariatric**: increasingly utilized for weight regain or insufficient initial response; semaglutide (Wegovy, Ozempic), tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound); response variable but many achieve meaningful additional weight loss; long-term data accumulating. **Multidisciplinary lifelong care** — essential for bariatric patient; integrate medical, surgical, nutritional, psychological, behavioral approaches.
reference_source: ASMBS Position Statements on Revisional Bariatric; SAGES Guidelines; Felsenreich DM et al. Obes Surg

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 60 ปี diagnosed with HCC 6 months ago — Child-Pugh B + advanced HCC + portal vein invasion + ascites + recently completed atezolizumab/bevacizumab but progression + ECOG 2 + พ่อแม่และบุตรหลายคน + ครอบครัวยอมรับยาก

คำถาม: integrative palliative + end-of-life care

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Advanced HCC progression with limited treatment options — integrative palliative + serious illness conversations**: (1) **Multidisciplinary team meeting**: medical oncology, hepatology, palliative care, social work, chaplain, family medicine; (2) **Goals of care discussion** with patient + family — serious illness conversation framework (Ariadne Labs / Atul Gawande): - Understanding of illness + prognosis - What's important to patient (values, fears, hopes) - What outcomes they want to avoid - What functional trade-offs acceptable - Family wishes + dynamics - Cultural + spiritual considerations; (3) **Prognostic estimation**: - Advanced HCC + Child B + progression on first-line + ECOG 2 → median survival 3-6 months - Be honest but compassionate — 'hope for the best, prepare for the worst' - Surprise question: 'would I be surprised if patient died in 12 months?' — useful prognostic question; (4) **Treatment options to consider** (decreasing intensity): - Second-line systemic therapy (lenvatinib, sorafenib, regorafenib, cabozantinib, ramucirumab if AFP > 400) — limited efficacy; weigh toxicity vs benefit; ECOG 2 borderline candidacy - Locoregional therapy (TACE, Y90, SBRT) — for liver-predominant; limited utility in advanced - **Best supportive care + palliative care** — focus on QOL + symptom management; (5) **Concurrent palliative care from diagnosis of advanced disease** (per Temel JCO 2010 NEJM 2010 — improves QOL + may extend survival); not 'palliative or curative' — both can coexist; (6) **Symptom management**: - **Pain**: WHO ladder; opioids — careful dose-adjust in hepatic impairment (avoid morphine accumulation in renal/hepatic, use hydromorphone or fentanyl; morphine OK at adjusted dose with monitoring); coadjuvants for neuropathic - **Ascites**: large-volume paracentesis as needed; sodium restriction + diuretic adjusted for hepatic dysfunction; PleurX catheter consideration for recurrent symptomatic - **Hepatic encephalopathy**: lactulose, rifaximin; treat precipitants - **Pruritus**: anti-histamine, cholestyramine, sertraline, naltrexone (caution) - **Nausea**: ondansetron, haloperidol, metoclopramide - **Fatigue**: address reversible (anemia, depression); pacing - **Anorexia / cachexia**: nutritional counseling; appetite stimulants limited efficacy (megestrol, mirtazapine, low-dose steroid); enteral if quality of life maintained - **Anxiety + depression**: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram), counseling - **Dyspnea**: oxygen, opioid (low-dose); (7) **Advance care planning + advance directive**: - Healthcare proxy designation - Living will - DNR / POLST discussion - Specific care preferences (intubation, dialysis, artificial nutrition); (8) **Hospice eligibility + referral**: - Medicare benefit if life expectancy ≤ 6 months + focus on comfort - Hospice services: comprehensive symptom management, nursing, social work, chaplain, volunteer, bereavement support, equipment, medications - Settings: home with hospice (preferred when feasible), inpatient hospice unit, residential, hospital; (9) **Family support**: - Anticipatory grief + counseling - Caregiver burden + respite - Family meeting facilitator (palliative care, social work) - Children's understanding (age-appropriate communication; child life specialists if hospital-based) - Cultural + spiritual rituals respected - Practical support (legal, financial, social services); (10) **Spiritual care**: chaplain consultation; cultural + religious practices integrated; (11) **Bereavement**: continue support for family for 12+ months after death; identify complicated grief; (12) **Care team self-care**: addressing 'second patient' issues — clinician grief, burnout prevention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stop all medications immediately + send home

explanation: **Advanced cancer integrative palliative care** — concurrent palliative care from diagnosis of advanced disease improves outcomes (Temel NEJM 2010 — early integrated palliative care in NSCLC improved QOL + mood + survival). **Serious illness conversation framework** (Ariadne Labs, Gawande): 1. Set up the conversation (permission, dedicated time) 2. Assess understanding + information preferences 3. Share prognosis (sensitive but honest) 4. Explore key topics: - What's important to you? - What are your fears + worries? - What goals do you have? - What outcomes are unacceptable? - How much are you willing to go through for the chance of more time? 5. Close the conversation (recommendation, plan) **Prognostication tools**: - Surprise question: 'would I be surprised if this patient died in 12 months?' - Performance status (ECOG, Karnofsky) - Disease-specific prognostic models - Palliative Performance Scale (PPS) - PaP (Palliative Prognostic) Score **Symptom management in end-stage liver disease + cancer**: - Pain (opioid dose adjustment): avoid morphine accumulation in renal/hepatic dysfunction; hydromorphone (renal/hepatic safer), fentanyl (hepatic OK, transdermal stable in cachectic), methadone (caution, prolonged QT, complex pharmacology) - Ascites: paracentesis, diuretic (judicious), albumin replacement, PleurX catheter for recurrent - Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose, rifaximin; treat precipitants (infection, GI bleed, dehydration, medications, constipation) - Pruritus: cholestyramine, sertraline, naltrexone, antihistamine - Anorexia / cachexia: nutritional support if QOL maintained; megestrol, mirtazapine, low-dose steroid (limited benefit); cannabinoids variable - Fatigue: address reversible (anemia, depression, hypothyroid, hypogonadism); methylphenidate selective benefit - Dyspnea: opioid (low-dose morphine 2.5 mg PO q4h or equivalent — central effect on dyspnea perception), oxygen, fan, positioning, anxiolytic **Advance care planning**: - Healthcare proxy designation - Living will - POLST / MOLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment) — actionable medical orders portable across settings - Code status discussion **Hospice eligibility (Medicare US)**: life expectancy ≤ 6 months + comfort-focused care + foregoing curative intent — hospice provides comprehensive interdisciplinary services (nursing, social work, chaplain, home health aide, bereavement, equipment, medications). **Family / caregiver support**: anticipatory grief, complicated grief screening, caregiver burden assessment, respite, social work, financial counseling, children's services (Child Life), bereavement support 12+ months. **Cultural + spiritual sensitivity**: language, religious practices, family hierarchy, decision-making customs, end-of-life rituals — integrate into care plan. **Clinician wellbeing**: burnout prevention, debriefing, peer support, mental health resources — recognized critical for sustainable palliative + serious illness care delivery.
reference_source: ASCO Palliative Care Integration; Temel JS et al. NEJM 2010; Ariadne Labs Serious Illness Care; AAHPM

scenario:
ชายไทยอายุ 55 ปี smoker, COPD GOLD C, ผ่า lung transplant 6 weeks ago — มา ED ด้วยอาการ shortness of breath progressive 3 days + fever 38.4°C + cough + decreased exercise tolerance

VS: BP 124/78, HR 102, RR 24, SpO2 88% room air
CXR: bilateral infiltrates new vs prior baseline
Lab: WBC 14,400 (was 8,000), Cr 1.4 (baseline 1.0), tacrolimus level 12 ng/mL (target 10-15)

คำถาม: differential + workup

choices.label: B
choices.text: **Post-lung transplant respiratory deterioration — broad differential, expedited workup**: (1) **Differential**: - **Acute rejection** (most common cause of decline first 6 mo) - cellular (T-cell mediated) more common than antibody-mediated - Diagnosis: bronchoscopy + transbronchial biopsy (gold standard) - Treatment: pulse steroid (methylprednisolone 1 g × 3 days), augment immunosuppression; ATG/rituximab for severe/AMR - **Infection** (also common): - CMV (q month surveillance for first 6-12 mo); treat valganciclovir/ganciclovir - PJP (Pneumocystis jirovecii — prophylaxis TMP-SMX, dapsone if intolerant); treat high-dose TMP-SMX + steroid - Fungal (Aspergillus most concerning in lung transplant) — voriconazole, posaconazole - Bacterial pneumonia (community + nosocomial) — culture-directed antibiotic - Atypical (Mycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydia) - **Reperfusion injury / primary graft dysfunction** — usually earlier (< 72h post-transplant) - **Airway complications**: anastomotic dehiscence, stenosis, malacia — bronchoscopy + intervention - **Pulmonary embolism** — high risk post-major surgery + hypercoagulable - **Cardiac**: rejection if heart transplant component, fluid overload - **Drug toxicity**: tacrolimus, mycophenolate, sirolimus pulmonary toxicity; (2) **Workup**: - CBC + diff, BMP, LFT, lactate, troponin, BNP - Respiratory cultures (sputum, bronchoscopy with BAL — culture for bacteria, virus, fungus, AFB) - CMV PCR (blood), galactomannan (Aspergillus), β-D-glucan - Tacrolimus + other drug levels (already checked — therapeutic) - ABG - CXR + CT chest with contrast (if Cr permits) - ECG, echo if cardiac concern - Bronchoscopy + transbronchial biopsy — for rejection vs infection; (3) **Empiric treatment** while workup in progress: - Broad-spectrum antibiotic (cefepime + vancomycin) - Antifungal if high risk (voriconazole) - CMV antiviral if suspected - Consider pulse steroid if rejection strongly suspected after infection ruled out; (4) **Multidisciplinary**: transplant pulmonology, ID, surgery, ICU; (5) **Long-term**: rejection prophylaxis (immunosuppression adherence, drug levels), infection prophylaxis (PJP, CMV, fungal), surveillance bronchoscopies, pulmonary rehab, vaccinations (inactivated), lifestyle

explanation: **Post-lung transplant respiratory deterioration** — broad differential; rejection + infection top considerations; both can overlap; biopsy often needed to distinguish. **Common causes by time after transplant**: - **< 30 days**: primary graft dysfunction, bacterial pneumonia, anastomotic issues, hyperacute rejection (rare with cross-match) - **30 days - 6 months**: acute cellular rejection peak, CMV, PJP (if not on prophylaxis), bacterial, Aspergillus, herpes - **6 months - 1 year**: late acute rejection, chronic rejection emerging (BOS — bronchiolitis obliterans syndrome / CLAD — chronic lung allograft dysfunction), community-acquired infections, CMV later if no prophylaxis - **> 1 year**: chronic rejection (CLAD/BOS) main long-term threat, infections, malignancy (PTLD, skin) **Acute rejection**: - **Cellular** (T-cell mediated) — more common; bronchoscopy + transbronchial biopsy (grading A0-A4 per ISHLT) - **Antibody-mediated (AMR)** — emerging recognition; DSA + biopsy with C4d staining; harder to diagnose **Bronchoscopy with BAL + transbronchial biopsy** — gold standard for: - Distinguishing rejection from infection - Identifying specific pathogen - Quantifying inflammation - Surveillance bronchoscopies routine first year **Common infections post-lung transplant**: 1. **CMV** — common; PCR surveillance; valganciclovir/ganciclovir prophylaxis + treatment 2. **PJP (P. jirovecii)** — TMP-SMX prophylaxis lifelong; high-dose TMP-SMX + steroid for treatment 3. **Aspergillus** — voriconazole prophylaxis often in early post-transplant; treatment voriconazole / isavuconazole / amphotericin 4. **Bacterial** — culture-directed; nosocomial vs community 5. **HSV / VZV / EBV (PTLD)** — antiviral prophylaxis (acyclovir/valacyclovir) **Chronic Lung Allograft Dysfunction (CLAD)** — main barrier to long-term survival post-lung transplant: - Bronchiolitis Obliterans Syndrome (BOS) — obstructive - Restrictive Allograft Syndrome (RAS) - 50% by 5 yr; limits long-term survival - Treatment options limited; some response to azithromycin (motility, anti-inflammatory), extracorporeal photopheresis, montelukast; re-transplantation in selected **Empiric treatment** while workup pending: balance treating likely conditions while waiting for tissue diagnosis; multidisciplinary team essential. **Drug interactions** — extensive: azoles (fluconazole, voriconazole, posaconazole) interact with calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) — increase levels; dose-adjust + monitor closely.
reference_source: ISHLT Lung Transplant Guidelines; AST/ASTS Lung Transplant Guidelines; Greer M et al. Curr Opin Organ Transplant
